next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

Vel counted the road south in levels.

The Spent Towns fell behind them over three days. South of the last managed farms, the trade road narrowed to dirt and the fields went from rich to merely serviceable. Early summer had settled into warmth, thick enough by noon to press sweat into cloth and leave salt at the collar when it dried. The air smelled like cut grain, drying mud, and the faint mineral bite of old Shaping left in stone too long.

Twenty-eight sat behind everything else.

Dren walked point. He read the track by habit: fresh ruts, local bootprints, a wheel that had listed left under too much weight, last night's soft spots where a wagon had cut deeper after dark. Twenty years on the southern routes showed in how little of his attention looked busy.

"There's a depot settlement about an hour ahead," he said. "Grain storage, one inn if they still call it that, water trough by the road. We fill up there and keep moving."

"How big?" Vel asked.

"Thirty, forty inside the fence. Farms all around it. Folks there count strangers the same way they count sacks."

Kem walked behind her with his own pack and, for once, did not fight the straps. The road had knocked a little of the careful city posture out of him. He still looked at the fields as if open land arrived in pieces he had to learn one by one.

"Is that wheat?" he asked, nodding to the nearest field.

"Barley," Dren said.

"Right."

"You can tell by the awns."

"I know. Vel told me."

Dren glanced back once. "Then start knowing it on sight. South of here, somebody will charge you extra if you make the...
open_hooks_to_carry: The Spent Towns as functional communities the Hoarded dismiss
Dren's introduction: practical, profane, unimpressed by birth
Kem seeing working people for the first time — people who choose to spend
Dren's daughter Mira and the economic cost of her illness
Kem's House signet draws looks in the Spent Towns — a tavern keeper sees it and her face closes; his first shame at the symbol

payoffs_to_land.description: The signet's social meaning outside the Vault — in the Spent Towns, the Hoarded crest is not respected but resented

prev_tail_excerpt:
...ou going to sleep."

"Eventually."

"You should rotate the packs again first. Kess won't make another day with that heel if she keeps the same weight."

Sable looked at the packs. He was right. Of course he was right. The road had spent the day appointing authorities she had not anticipated.

"Thank you," she said.

Chael shrugged once. "It's a pack."

They redistributed in silence. A little weight off Kess. Some off Tarrell, who had tried to carry embarrassment and notebook and had capacity for only two. More onto Chael, who accepted it without drama. Sable took additional rations onto her own back because she had led them into a queue with nothing to trade but labor and that fact felt measurable.

When the others finally lay down, she remained by the fire with the finished Form 7-C drying beside her knee.

At the bottom was a final box: *LESSONS FOR FUTURE DEPLOYMENT.*

Sable held the pencil over the line for a long time.

The Council had issued six charm cases and no practical money. It had provided an emergency provision that still required writing before survival. It had called a dismantled crossing manageable and assumed the road would pause for a seal. She had believed more of that packet than she should have, and the cost had come due in mud, lost time, torn skin, and the look on Kess's face when the heel wrap came off.

She wrote: *Initial operating assumptions require revision.*

She folded the form, put it back in the case with the Wayfinder, and listened to the southward pull continuing through her body while the fire settled and the team slept badly around her.
secondary_character_beats: Dren's agreement is explicitly transactional — the payment might cover Mira's healing, and this calculation is visible in his negotiation
state_deltas_required: Kem's privilege confronting lived reality; Dren's economic risk calculation against his family's needs
The world expands beyond the Hoarded/Spent binary; Dren introduces the human cost of level economics
